result:
If I could choose any superpower, it would not be flight or invisibility. It would be the ability to perfectly remember the scent of a moment. Not just the obvious fragrance—the roses or the rain—but the entire olfactory tapestry: the warm, dusty smell of sun on a paperback’s pages; the sharp, clean tang of a winter morning that feels like inhaling starlight; the faint, comforting scent of your grandmother’s laundry detergent lingering on a hug.

These ephemeral cocktails are the true time machines. The smell of a specific brand of sunscreen can slam me back to being eight years old, the gritty feeling of beach sand on my feet instantly restored. The damp-earth scent of a particular potting soil is my mother’s garden in spring. These are not memories recalled; they are realities briefly re-lived, bypassing the brain’s careful editing to touch the raw nerve of feeling.

We live in a world so dominated by the visual, yet our most powerful anchor is this invisible sense, wired directly to the emotional and memory centers of our minds. It is humble, often ignored until a whiff of something forgotten stops you in your tracks, heart aching with a joy or a sadness you can’t quite name.

So, I would collect them. I’d bottle the scent of my dog’s paws (like popcorn and grass). I’d save the aroma of a house after everyone has gone to bed, quiet and settled. I’d preserve the electric, ozone smell of a city right before a summer downpour. Because these are the unsung diaries of a life, written not in words, but in molecules of meaning.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
